content:
# Final Review Votes
**Producer:** agent:codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark-xhigh

## Summary

All three amendments identify concrete safety and correctness defects in run lifecycle handling: two are explicit race/signaling risks in `run stop`, and one is a real daemon crash-detection gap. They should be accepted.

## Votes

- `fr-5-df12fd3f`: Accept
  The race is real: capturing lease ownership after waiting allows a different process to acquire the lock, and reclaiming by owner alone can evict an active replacement run. The stale/ownership check must be tied to the original holder (and/or verified dead) before lock and pid cleanup.
- `fr-5-bee729a6`: Accept
  Using only numeric PID when sending signals is unsafe under PID reuse. The existing `proc_start_ticks` indicates process identity should be revalidated immediately before each signal, otherwise stop can terminate an unrelated process after reuse.
- `fr-5-79c8dac9`: Accept
  For daemon-owned runs there is no persisted pid marker, yet status/resume treats any valid lease as liveness. A hard-killed daemon leaves runs marked running until lease TTL expires, blocking recovery and reporting false-positive health. This is a genuine crash-detection hole.
